response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, I don't spend much time on social media, maybe just a few times each month when I'm checking what friends are up to or looking at photography pages for inspiration. But even with my limited exposure, the fake content problem really worries me. When I do scroll through, I see things that just don't feel right, and it bothers me because I want to know what's actually true. Working at the café here in Rome, I hear customers talking about news they saw online, and sometimes I wonder if what they're sharing is even real. It makes me anxious, honestly, because misinformation can spread so quickly and cause real harm. My approach has become quite careful and methodical. Before I believe anything I see online, I try to check if other sources are saying the same thing. I'll ask myself basic questions like who posted this, does it seem too extreme or designed just to make me angry, and can I find this information somewhere else that's trustworthy. My curious nature pushes me to dig a little deeper rather than just accepting things at face value. I've learned that if something makes me feel very emotional right away, that's usually a red flag to pause and verify. Since I value accuracy and doing things properly, I've made it a personal rule not to share anything unless I'm reasonably confident it's legitimate. I've seen how fake news can divide people and create unnecessary conflict, and I don't want to contribute to that. My partner and I sometimes discuss things we've seen online, and we've gotten into the habit of fact-checking together. I think the key is staying skeptical without becoming completely cynical. Yes, there's a lot of garbage out there, but there's also genuine information. The photography and cinema communities I follow tend to be more authentic, which helps. Ultimately, spending less time on these platforms probably protects me somewhat, but when I am there, I try to be thoughtful and responsible about what I consume and share. It takes effort, but it feels like the right thing to do.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 35-year-old barista in Rome with primary school education who spends minimal time on social media (few times monthly). His high conscientiousness (81) suggests he would approach the topic methodically and responsibly. His high neuroticism (96) indicates anxiety about misinformation and its consequences. His curiosity as a psychological feature drives him to investigate and verify information. His limited social media use shapes his perspective as somewhat removed but still concerned. His hobbies in photography and cinema provide positive online community examples. His high agreeableness (68) suggests concern for social harmony and not wanting to spread harmful content. His moderate openness and extraversion create a balanced, practical perspective rather than extreme views.
response_parsed.tone: concerned, thoughtful, methodical, slightly anxious, sincere, practical
